pairwiseRationales: A leans on a book-report line, "The song is about the weight of what is gone," and an invented door metaphor. B is true to the song — "His goddaughter calls every night," "his father, alone since the divorce" — with warm, varied rhythm and a sharp texture: "engineered to sound like joy and ringing hollow."

B leans on the book-report "The song is about..." and an over-repeated "closed door" motif. A states it plainly ("Something ended and he will not name it"), varies rhythm, ends on "As it was. As it was," and gives a specific texture: "engineered to sound like joy and ringing hollow."

pairwiseRationales: B knows the song: "His goddaughter calls every night," "a frightened voice asks why he is on the floor," "his father, alone for years now." A opens by restating its own lens ("Change as an unshared burden defines this moment") and runs flat, generic second-person that would fit any song.

A puts you in the room: "His goddaughter calls every night... until the night he stops answering," "asks what pills he is on." B opens by wearing its lens as decoration ("Change as an unshared burden defines this moment"), hedges in generic second-person ("feels deeply personal and isolating"), and flattens into clinical restatement ("without processing").

evidence: Change as an unshared burden defines this moment.
The world has shifted, and you know it cannot be the same as it was, but this new reality feels like a weight you carry alone.
Gravity holds you back, keeping you from moving forward, and you find yourself with nothing left to say about the past.
You want someone to hold out a hand, but you also refuse to talk about what happened.
Voices from outside try to reach you, but you remain behind a closed door, feeling unhelped and seeing your own solitude reflected in others.
A voice from outside tries to reach you, but you stay behind a closed door.
Gravity holds you back from moving forward.
You have nothing left to say about the past, even as you desire a hand to hold.
The world has fundamentally changed, and you know it cannot go back to "as it was."
This new reality is shared by "us," but the experience of it feels deeply personal and isolating.
Concerned voices call, asking why you are alone and what you are taking.
You ring for help, but no one comes.
You actively avoid discussing the past, preferring to move on without processing.
